text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we saw what we carry as new covenant Kohathites; the light of Christ, the truth of the gospel, the message of the Kingdom, the knowledge of the Father who is the only Almighty God. We saw that this light is covered to the eyes of those who are not searching, just as the lampstand was hidden under sea-cow leather during the journey. And we saw that we are carriers and not sources but ambassadors who deliver a message that did not originate with us. Today we go deeper. We look at how we carry the lampstand, what behaviour is required of those who bear it, and what it means that God Himself tends our wick so we can burn more clearly.

The Kohathites did not carry the lampstand carelessly. They carried it covered, ordered, and submitted to the instruction of the priests. There was a right way and a wrong way. Nadab and Abihu brought strange fire before Yahweh and died (Leviticus 10:1-2). There was no such thing as an acceptable alternative method when it came to the holy things. The manner of carrying was as important as the carrying itself. And this has everything to say to us about how we carry the light of Christ today.

Matthew 5:16 says: “Let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven.” Notice what gives glory to the Father; not impressive words alone, not doctrines won,
not polished presentations; but the good works that others can see. The light is carried in the visible, daily, ordinary behaviour of the believer. How we treat people. How we speak. How we respond when we are wronged. How we live when no one important is watching. These are the seven lamps of the lampstand burning before the world. Philippians 2:15 says we are to be “blameless and innocent, children of God without blemish in the midst of a crooked and twisted generation, among whom you shine as lights in the world.” The shining is not a performance. It is a character. It is what we are in the middle of the crooked generation around us.

1 Peter 2:12 says: “Keep your conduct among the Gentiles honourable, so that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may see your good deeds and glorify God on the day of visitation.” The world may speak against us. The sea-cow leather may be mocked. But the conduct; the visible, consistent, daily life of the carrier; will speak louder than any argument. Daniel carried the lampstand in Babylon for decades. Not with a loud voice but with a faithful life. Daniel 6:4 says his enemies “could find no ground for complaint or any fault, because he was faithful, and no error or fault was found in him.” That is a Kohathite carrying the lampstand well. The gold inside may be covered.
But the behaviour of the carrier reveals that something extraordinary is inside the leather.

There will be days when the lampstand feels heavy. When the gospel is unpopular. When the truth about the Kingdom and the Father is rejected, mocked, or treated with contempt. When the world finds our message foolish. In those days, the temptation is to put the lampstand down; to set aside the calling, to soften the truth, to make the message more appealing by removing what offends. But the Kohathite who put the lampstand down did not solve any problem. He simply left the Holy Place in darkness. The problem was never the weight. The problem was whether the carrier would keep walking.

Romans 1:16 gives us Paul’s resolve: “For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.” He is not ashamed of the sea-cow leather. He is not embarrassed by a message that the educated Greeks called foolishness and the religious Jews called a stumbling block (1 Corinthians 1:23). He kept carrying. And in 2 Timothy 4:2 he passed the same responsibility to Timothy: “Preach the word; be ready in season and out of season; reprove, rebuke, and exhort, with complete patience and teaching.” In season; when people want to hear it. Out of season; when they do not. The lampstand burns in both seasons. We carry in both.

Ezekiel 3:18-19 gives us the solemn weight of what it means to carry the light and stay silent: “If I say to the wicked, ‘You shall surely die,’ and you give him no warning, nor speak to warn the wicked from his wicked way, in order to save his life, that wicked person shall die for his iniquity, but his blood I will require at your hand. But if you warn the wicked, and he does not turn from his wickedness, he shall die for his iniquity, but you will have delivered your soul.” The Kohathite was responsible for the lampstand on his shoulder.
We are responsible for the light on ours. We cannot force anyone to receive it. But we are called to carry it faithfully and offer it to every person we encounter.

The priests tended the lampstand twice every day; morning and evening.
One of their essential tasks was to trim the wick. As the wick burned, it produced carbon at its tip, a black, charred deposit that built up over time and caused the flame to flicker, to smoke, and to burn dimly if left unattended.
The priests used golden scissors; pure gold wick trimmers to cut off the carbon so the lamp could burn cleanly and brightly again. The trimmed carbon was caught in golden trays and carried out of the Holy Place entirely.

The golden scissors show how God trims our wicks. The carbon on the wick speaks of the sin and the flesh that accumulates in our lives and dims the light we are called to carry. The compromise we allow, the bitterness we keep,
the pride that builds up quietly, the worldliness that settles in; all of it is carbon on the wick. The flame is still there. The oil is still there. But the carbon is causing the light to flicker and smoke rather than burn cleanly. And Yahweh, through Christ the High Priest of His people, trims the carbon formed in us. Hebrews 12:6 says: “For Yahweh disciplines the one He loves, and chastises every son whom He receives.”

When God trims us; through correction in the Word, through the discipline of difficult circumstances, through the voice of a brother or sister speaking truth, through the stripping away of what we have depended on too much; He is not punishing us out of anger. He is not putting the lamp out. He is cutting away exactly what is causing the light to burn poorly, so that the lamp can burn brighter and cleaner than before. The wick that is trimmed burns better.
The believer who submits to discipline shines more clearly. The carbon must go so the flame can grow. Hebrews 12:11 says: “For the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it.” The scissors hurt in the moment. The brighter flame is the result.

The priests tended the lampstand morning and evening; without fail, without exception, through every day of the tabernacle’s existence. This speaks of the continuous, daily attention God gives to the light in His people. He does not trim once and then leave us alone. He tends morning and evening.
Every morning as we come to the Word, He is trimming. Every evening as we examine our hearts before Him, He is trimming. The discipline of God is not occasional. It is the daily, faithful, loving attention of a Father who is determined that the light on our shoulders will not grow dim while the world around us is in darkness.

We are the Kohathites of the new covenant. The lampstand is on our shoulders. The light of Christ; the truth of the Father, the truth of the Kingdom, the truth of the gospel, is what we carry. It is covered to those who are not searching.
It is revealed by Christ through our words, our deeds, and our character to those who are. We did not light the flame. We do not sustain it by our own power. We simply carry it faithfully, morning and evening, through every season of the wilderness, never laying it down, always yielding to the golden scissors that keep us burning brightly. This is what it means to carry the lampstand.

title: Prayer
text:
Search me, O Yahweh, and know my heart; try me and know my thoughts; and see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting (Psalm 139:23-24). We bring ourselves before You today as the carriers of the lampstand, and we ask You to look at the wick. Where there is carbon; where sin and compromise and pride and flesh have built up and are causing the light to flicker; bring the golden scissors. We do not ask You to be gentle with what dims the light. We ask You to trim it completely. Hebrews 12:6 says You discipline those You love. We receive that love today in the form of the scissors. Make us burn more cleanly. Make us burn more brightly. May the light of Christ that is on our shoulders never be dimmed by what we have allowed to accumulate on the wick. May our words carry the truth of Your Kingdom faithfully. May our lives carry the light of Your character visibly. And may those who are genuinely searching for the light of God find it in us; not because we are impressive, but because the gold inside us is real.
In Christ’s name, amen.
